title: Make a small change test-first
objective: Practice a safe code change workflow using a tiny command-surface or policy example.
summary: This lesson turns the debugging map from lesson 07 into a small-change workflow. You will start with the desired behavior, choose the owning test file, make the smallest code change, and verify with targeted tests before the full gate.

sources.label: Developer guide: testing strategy and command-change workflow

beats.title: Start with the behavior, not the edit
beats.text: A safe change starts as a sentence: what should a user see, what should be accepted, or what should be rejected? Then pick the owning test file. Command shapes belong in `CLICommandRouterTests.swift`. Folder and working-directory rules belong in their domain tests. Lifecycle orchestration belongs in `LifecycleCoordinatorTests.swift`. Only after the red test describes the desired behavior do you open source files.

beats.prompt: A tiny change says: adding an Allowed Folder with a new access-mode alias should normalize to the existing canonical mode. Which test file should own the first red test?

choices.text: `Tests/SandCoreTests/CLICommandRouterTests.swift`, because all command arguments pass through the router.
`Tests/SandCoreTests/FolderPolicyTests.swift`, because access-mode normalization is a folder policy rule.
`docs/cli-reference.md`, because users will eventually see the accepted mode there.

beats.feedback: Start where the behavior lives. Docs may need refresh later, but the rule should be specified first in the policy test.

beats.title: Use router tests for command shapes
beats.text: `CLICommandRouterTests.swift` checks the public command surface: help, version, every v1 command shape, unsupported commands, and calls made across the application boundary. The `RecordingSandboxApplication` helper records requests without running lifecycle code. If a shape is outside v1, write a rejection test that proves the router fails before reading files or calling the application.

beats.prompt: A new supported CLI option needs to reach lifecycle code. Predict the boundary files you would expect to touch before the coordinator can use that new value.
beats.answer: Add or update a router test in `CLICommandRouterTests.swift`, parse the option in `CLICommandRouter.swift`, carry it through a request struct or method in `Sources/SandCore/CLI/SandboxApplication.swift`, update the test helper's recorded `AppCall`, then use the request in the smallest matching application or lifecycle code.
beats.explanation: Command parsing should not leak directly into domain code. The request struct is the typed application boundary between raw CLI arguments and orchestration.

beats.title: Use domain tests for behavior rules
beats.text: Not every change is a router change. `FolderPolicyTests.swift` owns Access Mode aliases, Guest Path defaults, duplicate Guest Path rejection, and overlapping Host Mac folder rejection. `WorkingDirectoryMapperTests.swift` owns Host Mac current-directory mapping and fallback warnings. These tests let you change rules without pretending a whole CLI command ran.

beats.prompt: Which expectations protect Workload Command opacity and v1 boundaries?

choices.text: `sand mybox run pi --model gpt-5 -- literal` reaches the application as the same workload argument array.
The router validates that `pi --model gpt-5` is a known safe Pi command before dispatching.
An ephemeral workload override after `--` preserves workload flags such as `--from` instead of treating them as sand options.
Shortcut shapes like `sand mybox pi` should become special cases because Pi is common.
Unsupported v1 surfaces such as omitted ephemeral options should fail before application/backend work starts.

beats.feedback: Opacity means sand routes workload arguments; it does not understand Pi, pytest, shell flags, or any other workload-specific syntax.

beats.instruction: Draft a red-green-refactor checklist for one hypothetical tiny change. Choose either a command-shape change or a policy-rule change. Include the desired behavior sentence, first test file, first source file, targeted test command, full verification command, and one boundary you will not cross.
beats.copyText:
Tiny change checklist

Desired behavior:
- 

Owning test file:
- 

First red test name:
- 

First source file to inspect/change:
- 

If command shape changes, boundary/request file:
- Sources/SandCore/CLI/SandboxApplication.swift or N/A

Boundary to preserve:
- Keep Workload Commands opaque / keep unsupported v1 shapes rejected / avoid docs until behavior is clear

Targeted verification:
- swift test --filter <TestClassOrMethod>

Full verification:
- make check

Evidence to record:
-
beats.signsOfSuccess: The checklist names one behavior, one owning test file, one smallest source area, a targeted `swift test --filter ...` command, `make check`, and a concrete boundary such as Workload Command opacity or unsupported v1 rejection.
hints: For command shapes, start with `CLICommandRouterTests.swift` and `CLICommandRouter.swift`.
For folder rules, start with `FolderPolicyTests.swift` and the folder policy module.
For data crossing from CLI to lifecycle, look for request structs in `SandboxApplication.swift`.
Do not begin by editing generated docs; lesson 09 covers documentation impact.

beats.title: Verify narrow, then wide
beats.text: Run the smallest useful test while iterating: `swift test --filter CLICommandRouterTests`, `swift test --filter FolderPolicyTests`, or an individual test method. When the focused behavior is green and the refactor is done, run `make check`. That full gate runs `swift test` and the documentation freshness check, catching cross-module or generated-doc impacts before the change is called done.

beats.recap: Small sand changes are safest when they begin with behavior and an owning test. Use `CLICommandRouterTests.swift` for command shape and boundary calls, domain tests for policy rules, request structs for data crossing into the application layer, and targeted tests before `make check`.
beats.readyPrompt: Ready to decide when a behavior change also needs documentation refresh?

referenceNeeds: Add a cheat-sheet entry mapping tiny change types to first test files: command shape → CLICommandRouterTests, folder rule → FolderPolicyTests, working directory rule → WorkingDirectoryMapperTests, lifecycle orchestration → LifecycleCoordinatorTests.
Add practice-bank prompts for drafting red-green-refactor checklists with exact files and commands.
Include a reminder that Workload Commands remain opaque and unsupported v1 command surfaces should be rejected explicitly.
